title: Series
summary: 9709 P1 series — binomial expansion, arithmetic and geometric progressions, and sums with PhET Expression Exchange.
learningObjectives: Expand (1 + x)ⁿ for positive integer n and use the binomial coefficients.
Find nth term and sum of arithmetic and geometric series.
Solve problems in context (e.g. compound interest as GP).

simpleExplanation.title: Patterns in sequences
simpleExplanation.summary: Arithmetic series add a constant difference; geometric series multiply by a constant ratio. Binomial expansion gives coefficients for (a + b)ⁿ.
steps: AP: u_n = a + (n−1)d; sum S_n = n/2(2a + (n−1)d) or n/2(a + l).
Binomial: (1+x)ⁿ = 1 + nx + n(n−1)x²/2! + … for integer n.
Identify a and r (or a and d) before choosing the formula.

sections.content: **Series** questions on P1 mix **recognition** (AP vs GP) with **algebra**. Binomial expansion links to **probability** on P5 — learn nCr notation early.
For binomial expansion of (2 + 3x)⁵, factor **2⁵** first: 2⁵(1 + 3x/2)⁵, then expand the bracket — Cambridge sets this trap every year.

steps.caption: Binomial expansion: (1 + x)ⁿ for |x| < 1 when n is not a positive integer.
steps.embedHint: Build (a + b)² expansions — compare with the standard pattern.

steps.caption: Arithmetic series: u_n = a + (n−1)d; sum S_n = n/2(2a + (n−1)d).
steps.embedHint: Generate successive terms with a common difference.

steps.caption: Geometric series: u_n = ar^(n−1); sum S_n = a(1−r^n)/(1−r) for r ≠ 1.
steps.embedHint: Multiply each term by r — see the geometric pattern.

steps.caption: Sum to infinity exists when |r| < 1: S_∞ = a/(1−r).
steps.embedHint: Set |r| < 1 — partial sums approach a limiting value.
